hook: If you died tomorrow, would anyone know where to find your will — or whether you even have one?
body:
If you died tomorrow, would anyone know where to find your will — or whether you even have one? Right now, searches for 'how to write a last will and testament' are spiking. That tells us a lot of people are thinking about this — but thinking about it and actually having one are very different things.

A will isn't just a legal document. It's the clearest way to tell your family exactly what you want done with your home, your savings, and everything else you've spent a lifetime building. Without one, a judge decides. Your family waits. And what you wanted may never happen.

You don't need a lawyer to find out where you stand. A 10-minute assessment tells you exactly which gaps to close — and what to do first.

Get your estate readiness score at willestateready.com — $21, 10 minutes.

hook: Most adult children assume their parents have a will. Most of the time, they're wrong.
body:
Most adult children assume their parents have a will. Most of the time, they're wrong. And the moment a parent passes without one, the family doesn't just grieve — they also inherit a legal mess that can take years and thousands of dollars to untangle.

Bank accounts get frozen. Property gets stuck in probate. Siblings who always got along suddenly don't. None of it is what your parent would have wanted, and all of it was preventable.

The hard part isn't the paperwork — it's knowing where the gaps are before a crisis forces the conversation. If your parents are in their 60s, 70s, or beyond, now is the right time to find out what's actually in place. You can even run the assessment yourself to understand what questions to ask them.

Get your estate readiness score at willestateready.com — $21, 10 minutes.

hook: If something happened to you and your partner tonight, who would raise your kids?
body:
If something happened to you and your partner tonight, who would raise your kids? If the answer isn't written down in a legal document, the court will decide — and they won't ask you what you would have wanted.

Guardianship is the part of estate planning that parents of young children tend to put off the longest. It feels heavy to think about. But not naming a guardian doesn't protect your kids from that scenario — it just removes your voice from it.

The good news is that getting clarity on this doesn't have to take long or cost a fortune. The first step is understanding where your family's plan actually stands right now — what's in place, what's missing, and what matters most to address first.

Get your estate readiness score at willestateready.com — $21, 10 minutes.

hook: Probate isn't just a legal formality — it's a public, expensive, time-consuming process that your family has to go through after you're gone.
body:
Probate isn't just a legal formality — it's a public, expensive, time-consuming process that your family has to go through after you're gone. In some states it takes months. In others, years. Attorney fees, court costs, and delays can eat into the estate you spent decades building.

The frustrating part is that probate is largely avoidable with the right planning. Beneficiary designations, titling, and trusts can all keep assets out of the probate process entirely — but only if they're set up correctly before you die.

Most people don't know which of their assets would go through probate and which wouldn't. That's exactly the kind of gap a readiness assessment is designed to surface — so you can fix it while you still can.

Get your estate readiness score at willestateready.com — $21, 10 minutes.

hook: A will only matters after someone dies. A power of attorney matters the moment they can't speak for themselves.
body:
A will only matters after someone dies. A power of attorney matters the moment they can't speak for themselves. And for aging parents, that moment can come without warning — a stroke, a fall, a sudden diagnosis — and if there's no document in place, their adult children can't legally make financial or medical decisions on their behalf.

Without a durable power of attorney, families often have to go to court to establish guardianship just to pay their parent's bills or authorize medical care. It's a painful process at an already painful time.

If your parents are in their 60s or older, this is one of the most urgent documents to have in place — and most families don't realize it's missing until it's too late to get it signed.

Get your estate readiness score at willestateready.com — $21, 10 minutes.

hook: Life insurance isn't an estate plan — but without it, your estate plan falls apart.
body:
Life insurance isn't an estate plan — but without it, your estate plan falls apart. For young families, it's the financial foundation everything else is built on. If you die with a mortgage, young kids, and no coverage, the plan you put in place for your family may not be executable — because the money simply isn't there.

With Social Security policy in flux and retirement accounts increasingly under discussion in tax debates, the one thing young families can control right now is making sure the basics are locked in. Life insurance, a will, named guardians, and updated beneficiaries. That's the core.

Knowing where your family's plan stands — and what's actually missing — is a better starting point than guessing. The assessment takes 10 minutes and tells you exactly what to address first.

Get your estate readiness score at willestateready.com — $21, 10 minutes.

hook: A trust isn't just for wealthy people — it's for anyone who wants their family to avoid probate, protect privacy, and actually receive what they're supposed to.
body:
A trust isn't just for wealthy people — it's for anyone who wants their family to avoid probate, protect privacy, and actually receive what they're supposed to. With ongoing conversations about taxing capital gains to fund Social Security and shifting retirement tax rules, more people in their 50s and 60s are realizing that how assets are held matters just as much as how much they have.

A revocable living trust can pass assets directly to your beneficiaries without going through court. It can also provide clear instructions for what happens if you become incapacitated before you die — something a will alone can't do.

If you've been wondering whether a trust makes sense for your situation, the first step is understanding what your estate plan actually looks like right now — and what it's missing.

Get your estate readiness score at willestateready.com — $21, 10 minutes.
